params.text:
Recognising a seizure

Epilepsy is a common condition, and a person may also have a seizure for other reasons, such as a very high temperature or low blood sugar. In a generalised seizure, someone may suddenly fall, go stiff and then make jerking movements, may not respond to you, and may lose control of their bladder or bowel.

A seizure can look frightening, but most stop on their own within a few minutes. Your job is not to stop the seizure but to keep the person safe while it runs its course and to know when extra help is needed.

What to do and what not to do

Stay calm and protect the person from harm.

  • Protect them: clear the space around them, cushion their head, and move away anything hard or sharp.
  • Note the time: time how long the seizure lasts, as this affects whether you call 999.
  • Do not restrain: never hold them down, and never put anything in their mouth.
  • Afterwards: once the jerking stops, place them in the recovery position and reassure them gently as they come round.

Call 999 if a seizure lasts more than five minutes, if one seizure follows another without recovery, if it is their first ever seizure, or if they are injured or do not wake up.

params.question: Someone is having a seizure on the floor, with jerking movements of their arms and legs. What should you do?

answers.text: Move objects away, cushion their head, and let the seizure run its course while timing it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Protecting them from injury and timing the seizure is safe practice; you only intervene further if it lasts too long or they are hurt.

answers.text: Hold their arms and legs firmly to stop the shaking.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. You should never restrain someone during a seizure, as it can cause injury; let the movements happen safely.

answers.text: Put a spoon or your fingers in their mouth so they cannot bite their tongue.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Never put anything in the mouth of someone having a seizure — it can cause serious injury to them or to you.

answers.text: Lift them onto a nearby chair so that they are more comfortable.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Moving someone mid-seizure risks injury; keep them where they are, cushion the head and clear the area.
